coredata.dc:title: Violuric acid carbon dots as a highly fluorescence probe for ultrasensitive determination of Zn (II) in tomato paste
coredata.dc:creator: Abdulsatar Esmail, Lawen
Sanaan Jabbar, Hijran
coredata.dc:description: Zinc is an essential metal since it plays an important role in biological systems, therefore, determination of zinc in food samples is important. Violuric acid was used to prepare highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), when it irradiated with ultraviolet radiation at 365 nm, a strong violet fluorescence was observed which caused by the increased amount of nitrogen in the CD structure, which were then successfully used for sensing zinc ion based on quenching of fluorescence. Violuric acid's hydrothermal carbonization reaction's temperature and time were simply optimized for better-quality performance of the CDs as-synthesized. The probe was characterized by HRTEM, SEM, XRD, EDX, fluorescence, UV–Visible absorption spectrophotometry, and FTIR. With a lower LOD 0.32 nM, the developed approach demonstrates an exceptional sensitivity and good selective response to the Zn2+ at 25℃. Compared to the results from ICP, the sensor was successfully used for determination of Zn2+ ions in tomato paste samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: New carbon dots
Violuric acid
Fluorescent nanomaterial
Zinc ion
Tomato paste

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal process for preparation of carbon dots
Calibration curve and determination of Zn (II)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration showing the synthesis of CDs from violuric acid and the determination of Zn (II) ion in tomato paste.
UV–Visible absorption spectra of CDs at different temperatures and (a) different time of reaction with 180 ℃ (b).
Characterization of CD: a) HRTEM image, b) scale distribution histogram (inset), c) SEM image, d) XRD, e) FTIR spectrum, and f) EDX spectra.
Fluorescence spectrum of CD (a) excited from 280 to 400 nm, with 10 nm wavelength intervals; (b) quenching effects of 900 nM Zn2+ ions.
Different metal ions effect on the intensity of new carbon dot probe's fluorescence.
Analytical performance comparison of several CDs for sensing of Zn2+ ions.

row.entry: (Han, Peng, Yu, Chi, Li, Hu, et al., 2018)
(Song, Ma, Wang, Tang, Song, Ma, et al., 2018)
(Yang, Tang, Meng, Liu, Feng, Zhao, et al., 2018)
(da Silva Júnior, Macuvele, Riella, Soares, & Padoin, 2021)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for zinc ions determination obtained from various tomato paste samples.

head.ce:title: Violuric acid carbon dots as a highly fluorescence probe for ultrasensitive determination of Zn (II) in tomato paste

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc is an essential metal since it plays an important role in biological systems, therefore, determination of zinc in food samples is important. Violuric acid was used to prepare highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), when it irradiated with ultraviolet radiation at 365 nm, a strong violet fluorescence was observed which caused by the increased amount of nitrogen in the CD structure, which were then successfully used for sensing zinc ion based on quenching of fluorescence. Violuric acid's hydrothermal carbonization reaction's temperature and time were simply optimized for better-quality performance of the CDs as-synthesized. The probe was characterized by HRTEM, SEM, XRD, EDX, fluorescence, UV–Visible absorption spectrophotometry, and FTIR. With a lower LOD 0.32 nM, the developed approach demonstrates an exceptional sensitivity and good selective response to the Zn2+ at 25℃. Compared to the results from ICP, the sensor was successfully used for determination of Zn2+ ions in tomato paste samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Various chemicals may enter the food chain by means of unintentional and intentional addition. Numerous compounds, such as micronutrients, flavoring substances, mycotoxins, environmental contaminants, pesticide residues, and food additives, are among these chemicals. Tin, cadmium, and lead are nonessential metals since they are harmful even in trace levels, whereas metals like zinc, copper, and iron are necessary because they play a crucial part in biological processes. Even at low concentrations, toxic elements can be seriously dangerous if consumed over an extended period of time. When consumed in excess, the essential metals can potentially have harmful consequences. The use of tomato paste is widespread across the world. Tomato paste is frequently used to add taste and color to a variety of food products. Due to the composition, they have a significant nutritional value in addition to their flavor attributes. Studies on public health often include trace element analysis because of how importance it is to human metabolism (Yenisoy-Karakaş, 2012).

Carbon dot (CD), also known as carbon quantum dot (CQD), carbon nano-particles (CNPs), and fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs), are increasingly prevalent study subjects at the moment and have drawn a lot of interest. Nano carbon materials are composed of carbogenic cores that are smaller than 10 nm in dimensions and include functional groups (amine, carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc.) around them. It possesses a wide range of distinguishing characteristics, such as size-dependent optical properties like electrochemical and fluorescence fields, high resistance against photobleaching chemical inertness, aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, high flexibility in modification, great elasticity in modification, biocompatibility, and aqueous solubility. They have discovered a wide range of application areas, including chemical sensing, delivery of drug, catalysis, and bioimaging. Several classes of chemicals, that includes metal ions, explosives, small organic molecules, pesticides, protein and nucleic acid, and bacteria, have been effectively studied employing CDs-based sensors as sensing sources (Othman, Hassan, & Faizullah, 2021). Numerous processes, including hydrothermal, microwave, ultra-sonication chemical oxidation, and others are used to synthesis of CDs (da Silva Júnior, Macuvele, Riella, Soares, & Padoin, 2021).

These materials have reduced particle sizes, low toxicity, photo induced electron transfer, excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence behavior, chemical inertness, and high biocompatibility, making them potential candidates for nano-biotechnology. These materials offer better fluorescent properties than conventional fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots, including high photo-stability against photo blinking and bleaching, controllable and narrow emission spectra, and broad excitation spectra (Das, Bandyopadhyay, & Pramanik, 2018).

Many fluorescent probes have been created in the last 20 years and effectively utilized to detect various analytes, including proteins, DNA, molecules, and ions (Liu, Wolstenholme, Carter, Liu, Hu, Grainger, et al., 2018; Shen, Zhang, Sun, Zhao, Ye, Xu, et al., 2017). Most fluorescence studies were based on single signal changes, such as enhancement or quenching of the fluorescence (Ganganboina, Dutta Chowdhury, & Doong, 2018). However, the stability and precision of the instrument, fluorescence probe concentration, and background noises can all interfere with these fluorescence analyses (Zou, Foda, Tan, Shao, Wu, Lu, et al., 2016). Zinc is an important trace element in the human body, which appears as the divalent cation Zn(II) ions (Bothra, Babu, Paira, Ashok Kumar, Kumar, & Sahoo, 2018). Numerous human activities, such as neurophysiology, metalloenzyme regulation, cell apoptosis, RNA transcription, DNA and peptide synthesis, etc., can be supported by Zn(II) ions (Kim, Hwang, Jang, Kang, Kim, Noh, et al., 2013). As a result, different fluorescent probes were utilized to measure the amounts of Fe(III) and Zn(II) ions (Hashemi, Vaezi, Sedghi, & Naderi-Manesh, 2018; Shangguan, Huang, He, He, Wang, Ye, et al., 2017). Hashemi and colleagues created a unique water-soluble Fe-quantum cluster incorporated into hemoglobin, cysteine and Zn(II) ions to induce color changes in visible region and fluorescence “switch on” of the Fe-quantum clusters solution (Hashemi, Vaezi, Sedghi, & Naderi-Manesh, 2018).

Because of it is importance in human health, a new and highly photoluminescent CDs synthesized from violuric acid using one-pot hydrothermal process for the determination of Zn2+ ions in tomato paste samples. There is no publication on the detection of zinc ions from carbon dots made with amino functional groups derived from violuric acid. The CDs' fluorescence was quenched as a result of the zinc ions' coordination with their functional groups. Zinc ions can be detected through fluorescence quenching of carbon dots.

The analytical grade of all compounds allowed for their use without additional purification. All studies were conducted using deionized water. Violuric acid monohydrate was obtained from Sigma. Others chemicals obtained from Sigma Aldrich include NaCl, KCl, AgNO3, ZnCl2, MgSO4⋅7H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CuCl2·2H2O, NiCl2⋅6H2O, CaCl2, HgCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·9H2O, CoCl2⋅6H2O, and CrCl3⋅6H2O. Buffer solution of phosphate (pH 9.0) was prepared from Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 (0.01 mol/L). Zinc stock solution (1.0 µM) was prepared from ZnCl2 salt (Merck). While repeated dilution of the stock solution was used to get lower concentrations.

A Biochrom LKB spectrophotometer was used to record the UV–vis absorbance spectra. A Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer was used to measure fluorescence (Kattan Co.). Utilizing a transmission electron microscope, HRTEM images were captured (PHILIPS, CM120, Netherland). The Perkin-Elmer spectrometer was used to record FTIR spectra at room temperature. Freeze-drying carbon dots powderused with PW, 1730-Philips instrument to create the XRD pattern.

EDX systems are attachments to Electron Scanning Microscopy (SEM) used for EDX analysis. The EDX analysis with 15 kV accelerated voltage was done. A small amount of freeze drying carbon dots was attached to sample holder, the sample was then coated with extremely thin layer of gold using TESCAN MIRA III (Czech) SEM coating system machine. Then a ray of high energy electrons is focused to the sample to create signals from the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal process for preparation of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal approach using violuric acid as carbon precursor according to the reported methods (Y. Kim & Kim, 2020) with some modification. First, 0.8 g of violuric acid with 50 mL of deionized water in a beaker and stirred for 10 min. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 5.0 h in an oven. The reaction product was gradually cooled to room temperature afterward. Finally, the suspension was subjected to centrifugation (8000 rpm) for 15 min and passed through a 0.2 µm micro syringe filter then stored at 4.0 ℃ for further uses. The same procedure was repeated at 150, and 220 ℃ to reach the optimum temperature for synthesis of CDs. Fig. 1 illustrates the synthesis of CDs from violuric acid and Zn (II) determination in tomato paste.

The Zn2+ ion detection was performed at 25 ℃ (room temperature) in a phosphate buffer pH = 9.0 with a typical run 200.0 μL of purified CDs solution and varying amounts of zinc chloride solution (The concentration range was 0.5–900 nM) were separately added into the cuvette and diluted with phosphate buffer to 4 mL and FL emission spectra were recorded. After the usual incubation, the FL measurement was performed at intervals of 5 min. For the selectivity study, 5.0 μL of different metal ions (10 nM) was added to CDs solution and fluorescence quenching was compared. After 5.0 min of mixing, the fluorescence spectra were captured at 330 and 410 nm of excitation, and emission wavelengths with a 10 nm slit width (da Silva Júnior, Macuvele, Riella, Soares, & Padoin, 2021; Wang, Lao, Ding, Zhang, & Liu, 2019; Manman Yang, Kong, Li, Liu, Huang, Liu, et al., 2015).

Samples were prepared according to the reported literature (Boadi, Mensah, Twumasi, Badu, & Osei, 2012) with some modifications. 10 mL of concentrated HNO3 was poured to a 200 mL beaker after adding 2 g of tomato paste sample. The samples were left to dissolve in the acid overnight while the beaker was covered with a watch glass. On a hot plate, the dissolved sample was heated until all active reactions ceased and a clear solution was produced. The digest was taken off the hot plate, let to cool, and then filtered. The filtered solution was put into a 50 mL volumetric flask and completed with deionized water to the appropriate concentration.

The quantum yield (Q.Y) of carbon dots was determined according to an established procedure. Briefly, quinine sulfate in 0.10 mol/L sulfuric acid (Q.Y: 0.540 at 360 nm) was chose as the standard. To minimize the effects of re-absorption, absorbencies in the 10.0 mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.050 at the excitation wavelength of (360 nm). Using the following equation, the Q.Y of the carbon dots was calculated:
Q . Y =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n
2
n
R
2
where (Q.Y) is the quantum yield, (I) is the measured integrated fluorescent emission intensity, (n) the solvents refractive index, and A is the absorbance. The subscript R refers to the corresponding parameter of known fluorescent standard.

The single precursor used in the synthesis of CDs is violuric acid. The synthesized carbon dots were well dispersed in water and formed a solution with yellowish brown color. The time and temperature of hydrothermal carbonization reaction of violuric acid were optimized to provide a better-quality performance for as-synthesized carbon dots (Sahoo, Jana, Aktara, Das, Nayim, Patra, et al., 2020). The carbon dots synthesized at various temperatures (150℃, 180℃, and 220℃) in order to get the most optimal condition at the moment of synthesis. UV–vis spectra were used to characterize all of the produced CDs solutions, and it was found that 180 ℃ was the optimal temperature for CD synthesis (Fig. 2 a). It was found that the spectra of CDs formed two distinct peaks at 250 nm and 280 nm when the temperature increased from 150 to 180℃, similar result obtained by Alarfaj et al. (Alarfaj, El-Tohamy, & Oraby, 2018). The peak at 250 nm may be explained by the aromatic CC and CC bonds π-π* transition, which emerged from the aromatic π system (Liu, Jia, Li, Li, Yu, Wang, et al., 2019). Due to the successful oxidation of carbon-based NPs, a peak at 285 nm might be ascribed to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl groups (CO). Under UV (365 nm) lighting, extremely vivid purple luminescence could be noticed as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 2a.

Prior to separating the finished product during the synthesis of carbon dots, it is crucial to ensure that the conversion of violuric acid into carbon dots has achieved its completion under the experimental conditions. To investigate this further, the conversion process was observed over time at 180℃, and its UV–vis spectra were periodically recorded and compared with the UV–vis spectra of violuric acid that give maximum absorbance at 545 nm (Fig. S1a). The 3.0 h result (Fig. 2b) indicated that the conversion rate is low. The conversion rate rises as the time increases, reaching its maximum peak at five hours. This implies that all biomass-convertible has been transformed into CDs and there is not any peak at 545 nm which indicates the complete conversion of violuric acid into CDs, and that when the reaction time is increased by another 10 h, the rate of carbonization marginally declines. This is because the functional groups that are present on the surface of carbon dots are broken down with longer reaction times. When the C-dots aqueous solution was irradiated with ultraviolet radiation at a wavelength of 365 nm, a strong violet fluorescence was observed which caused by the increased amount of nitrogen in the CD structure (Hola, Sudolská, Kalytchuk, Nachtigallová, Rogach, Otyepka, et al., 2017).

Different HRTEM pictures were used to determine the morphologies and particle sizes for as-synthesized carbon dots, and image J software was used to determine the size distribution. The HRTEM image (Fig. 3 a, and Fig. S2) is evident of dense population of formed NP with most of particle acquiring spherical shape (Lu, Li, Jia, Ma, Jia, Tao, et al., 2016). The histogram shown in Fig. 3b shows the distribution of particle in the range of 0.5–1.6 nm. CDs average size in this case is estimated to be 0.9 nm. Also SEM image (Fig. 3c) shows that the morphology of the CD is roughly spherical shape (W. Lu, Shen, Xie, & Zhang, 2010).

The XRD pattern for CDs exhibits poor crystalline nature with a broad peak centered at ∼ 25° synchronizing to the (002) (Fig. 3d), signifying the amorphous nature of the as-synthesized CDs (Zhao, Li, Liu, Liu, Qiao, Han, et al., 2015). The interlayer spacing of the C-dots (d) was calculated as 0.2 nm. Additionally, the distance between the two lattice planes measured in HR-TEM and the XRD results were in good agreement (Arumugam & Kim, 2018).

The FTIR spectrum of the CD is shown in Fig. 3e. Several distinctive bands connected to the surface functional groups can be seen. Among them the peak observed at 3560 cm−1 was attributed to the OH stretching vibration (Tarokh, Pebdeni, Othman, Salehnia, & Hosseini, 2021), and that at 3193 cm−1 to the NH stretching vibration (Huang, Li, Chen, Tong, Lin, Zhu, et al., 2018). The peak observed at 2853 cm−1 was attributed to the CH stretching vibration (Lv, Man, Dong, Huang, & Wang, 2020; Sahoo, et al., 2020). The peaks at 1424 cm−1 and 1713 cm−1 are attributed to CC and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1622 cm−1 was attributed to –COOH stretching vibration (Han, Wang, Wang, Xu, Sui, Li, et al., 2016; Xu, Yang, Lan, Zhang, Peng, Jin, et al., 2016). The peak at 1332 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibrations of CN (Arumugam & Kim, 2018; Lv, Man, Dong, Huang, & Wang, 2020). The band at 1067 corresponds to the CO stretching (Y. Kim & Kim, 2020). The CN and CO stretching and –OH bending vibrations are responsible for the additional strong peaks between 1000 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 (Lv, Man, Dong, Huang, & Wang, 2020). The band at 843 cm−1 corresponds to the NH bending vibrations (Li, Ou, Huang, Wu, Chen, Lin, et al., 2014). From the observation above, it can be infer that the purchased CDs had CO, CO, CH, -OH/NH, bonds functionalized on them (Sahoo, et al., 2020).

The elemental composition of carbon dots determined by EDX analysis, and the results shows presence of C (23.33 %), N (30.35 %), O (43.27 %), Na (0.72 %), P (0.34 %), S (0.62 %), Cl (0.32 %), and Zn (1.06 %) as in Fig. 3f. According to the elemental composition the as synthesized carbon dots composed mostly of O, N and C atoms which is indicated the formation of carbon dots. A minor amount of Na, P, S, Cl and Zn was also found due to the use of violuric acid, which contain this element. (Murru, Romero, Sánchez-Mudarra, García Ruiz, Morales, Capitán-Vallvey, et al., 2020; Pandey, Rawat, Prasad, & Bohidar, 2020; Patir & Gogoi, 2019; Yahaya Pudza, Zainal Abidin, Abdul Rashid, Md Yasin, Noor, & Issa, 2019).

The carbon dots exhibited typical excitation-dependent fluorescence emission characteristics, which means wavelength-tunable emission, like many CDs described earlier (Fig. 4 a). When the CDs were stimulated by the photo, the emission wavelength fluctuated slightly, ranging from 280 to 330 nm. When the excitation wavelength was changed from 330 nm to 400 nm, it was evident that the emissions' intensity had been redshifted, which supported the CDs' considerable ability for wavelength tuning. The CDs were stimulated at a wavelength of 330 nm, which provided the highest emission intensity (Lv, Man, Dong, Huang, & Wang, 2020; Sahoo, et al., 2020). It is clear from Fig. 4b that adding Zn2+ caused the fluorescence intensity to drop down quickly. The presence of –COOH, –NH2, and –OH on the surface of carbon dots that were chelated or coordinated with Zn2+ may have contributed to the strong fluorescence-quenching effect by causing the excited state electrons of the carbon dots to transfer to the unoccupied 4S orbital of Zn2+ and resulting in non-radiative electron/hole recombination (Huang, et al., 2018; Y. Kim & Kim, 2020). As a consequence, the outcome demonstrated that the zinc ion causes carbon dots to lose their fluorescence, which may be attributed to the coordination of closeness between the functional groups present in the CD and the metal under these conditions (Carvalho, Santos, Germino, Terezo, Moreto, Quites, et al., 2019). Quantum yield Q.Y (Φ) measurement was conducted according to the previously published papers by comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensities of quinine sulfate that chosen as a standard and synthesized CDs. The carbon dots with sizes ranging from 0.5 to 1.6 nm were emitted a strong violet emission with quantum yield measured to be 73.20 %. The decay lifetime was determined to be 8.4 ns for the obtained violet-emitting CDs fluorescence.

The quenching response between carbon dot and Zn2+ions and the fluorescence intensity were examined as a function of sensor conditions such as pH and incubation time. The fluorescence response and degree of quenching were investigated at various pH levels (3, 5, 7, 9, and 12) in the absence and presence of 1.0 nM Zn2+. Due to their effects on the CDs that are luminescence source and the analyte Zn2+, which results in poorer quenching efficiency (F˚/F), differing pH levels often have a unique impact on the intensity of fluorescence. Fig. S3a demonstrates the at low pH values quenching efficiency is low, rises to its maximum at pH = 9, and subsequently falls as pH increases.

The surface of the CD is covered with functional groups including hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and amine; a change in pH can affect how these groups interact with Zn2+ ions by altering their charge. Another method that increasing pH might modify the quenching ability is via hydroxide ions forming coordination complexes with Zn2+ ions. The ability of quenching can also alter when hydroxide (OH) ions form coordination complexes with Zn2+ ions at higher pH levels (Barati, Shamsipur, & Abdollahi, 2016; Yahyazadeh & Shemirani, 2019). The pH value of 9 produced the highest quenching efficiency (F˚/F), hence this was chosen for further research.

Additionally, several time intervals were used to analyze the incubation period, or the time needed for the carbon dot and Zn (II) ions to completely react (1 – 60 min). The carbon dots quenching reaction by 1.0 nM Zn (II) started after 1.0 min, reached its highest value after 5.0 min, and then remained mostly steady for an additional 60 min, as shown in Fig. S3b. It was concluded that the quenching reaction, which only took 5 min to complete, was rather quick and was selected as the ideal period for incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve and determination of Zn (II)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of the as-synthesized CDs in the presence of various zinc ion concentrations were presented in Fig. S4a in order to assess the sensitivity and application of the fluorescent sensing probe toward Zn2+ ions. It was clear that the fluorescence intensity at 410 nm gradually decreased as the quantity of Zn2+ ions rose. Plotting the quenching efficiency (F˚/F) against varied Zn2+ ions concentrations in the range of 0.50–900 nM resulted in a good linear connection, as illustrated in Fig. S4b. The derived linear regression equation is F˚/F = 0.0022C + 1.5321, where F and F˚ stand for the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots in the presence and absence of Zinc2+ ions, respectively, and the concentration of Zn2+ ions represented by C, with the associated regression coefficient of (R2 = 0.9943). The limits of detection (LD = 3SDb/Ks) was determined to be 0.32 nM using curve slope of the quenching efficiency (Ks) and blank solutions standard deviation (S.Db) (n = 5).

The precision and accuracy of the approach were analyzed statistically (intra- and inter-day) in order to determine the chemosensor's competence. Five replicate determinations of three Zn2+ standard solutions (100, 200, and 600 nM) were performed using obtained optimal conditions to assess relative standard deviation (RSD) as precision. While calculating the relative error (E %), which was used to assess the accuracy of the method, the results were presented in Table S1. The positive outcomes show that the established methodology has excellent accuracy and precision. As demonstrated in Table 1 , this sensing method works better than other reported fluorescent approaches for determining Zn(II) in terms of wide linear range, lower limits of detection, and sensitivity.

Selectivity is one of the most important factors determining any methods efficacy, which is equally crucial to sensitivity. In light of this, several metal ions effect, including Cr (II), Pb (III), Fe (II), Fe (III), Cd (II), Cu (II), Ni (II), Co (II), K (I), Ag (I), Mg (II), and Na (I), on the intensity of fluorescence was observed. How each linked metal affected CD's fluorescence intensity on its own was considered.

Each associated metal's influence on CD's fluorescence intensity was examined independently. The influence of the other ions, with the exception of Zn (II), may be disregarded. Only Zn (II), as seen in Fig. 5 , caused a very strong quenching of photoluminescence (compared with the other metal ions). This demonstrates the remarkable Zn (II) selectivity of the CDs probe over other metal ions (Arumugam & Kim, 2018).

The effectiveness of the fluorescent probe CD for the determination of zinc ion in real samples was tested using six samples of tomato paste namely Al ain 500 g (UAE), Altunsa 450 g (Turkye), Harir 250 g (Iraq), Mosaffa 250 g (Iran), Nawras 450 g (Nawras Group, Turkey), ZER 450 g (ZER Group, Turkey). The probe was successfully used under ideal experimental conditions to quantify Zn2+ in samples of various tomato paste, the obtained results were given in Table 2 . When the obtained results of this approach were compared to those of ICP, no statistically significant differences were found.

A highly photoluminescent carbon dot-based sensing probe with enhanced selectivity and sensitivity has been developed. The synthesis process has been carried out using a straightforward hydrothermal technique. The CDs were amorphous and their photoluminescent emission was excitation-wavelength-dependent. The probes exhibit strong fluorescence at 410 nm when exposed to UV light. The CDs demonstrated a robust reaction to the presence of Zn2+ ions by quenching fluorescence light. With its low limits of detection (0.32 nM) and wide linear range, the newly designed sensor might be used as a tool to estimate zinc ions. The most important advantages of the proposed method is its extra-high sensitivity when compared to the reported methods.
No funding was received for this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135638.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
